volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: Pseudolus (Section 4.7.1170b - Section 4.7.1228b)

sections.label: Section 4.7.1170b
sections.translation: With the victory of his prowess in battle ; for I was a very great general at home in my own country .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1172
sections.original: An etiam umquam ille expugnavit carcerem , patriam tuam ?
sections.translation: Why , did he ever capture a gaol in your country ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1173
sections.translation: If you utter affronting speeches , you ’ ll be hearing them .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1173b
sections.translation: In what time did you come from Sicyon hither ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1174b
sections.translation: In one day , by noon .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1175
sections.translation: Bravo ! By my troth , you did come quickly ;

sections.label: Section 4.7.1175b
sections.translation: how very swift of foot this man is .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1176
sections.original: ubi suram aspicias , scias pósse eum — gerere crassas compedes .
sections.translation: When you look at his calves , you might know that he can wear heavy fetters .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1177
sections.original: Quid ais ? tune etiam cubitare solitu ’ (s e) s in cunis puer ?
sections.translation: How say you — were you also , when a child , in the habit of sleeping in a cradle ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1178
sections.translation: Of course .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1178b
sections.original: Etiamne facere solitus es — scin quid loquar ?
sections.translation: And were you , too , in the habit of doing [*] — you know what I mean ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1179
sections.translation: Of course he was in the habit of doing as other children did ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1179b
sections.translation: Are you men in your senses ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1179c
sections.translation: What , because I asked you that ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1180
sections.original: noctu in vigiliam quando ibat miles , quom tu ibas simul ,
sections.translation: At night , when the Captain was going on guard , and when you were going with him , did the sword of the officer fit your scabbard ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1182
sections.translation: Go to utter perdition .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1182b
sections.original: Ire licebit tamen tibi hodie temperi .
sections.translation: You , indeed , shall have the opportunity of going there in good time to-day .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1183
sections.original: Quin tu mulierem mi emittis , aut redde argentum .
sections.translation: But do you bring out the woman to me , or else give me back the money .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1183b
sections.translation: Wait a bit .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1184
sections.translation: Why should I wait ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1184b
sections.translation: That scarf , tell me for how much it has been lent .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1184c
sections.translation: What matters it ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1185
sections.translation: How much is the sword hired for ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1185b
sections.translation: These fellows surely stand in need of hellebore [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1185c
sections.translation: How now —

sections.label: Section 4.7.1186
sections.translation: Leave me alone .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1186b
sections.original: Quid mercedis petasus hodie domino demeret ?
sections.translation: What wages does that broad-brimmed hat hire to-day for its owner ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1187
sections.original: Quid domino ? quid somniatis ? mea quidem haec habeo omnia ,
sections.translation: [*] What owner ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1188
sections.translation: What are you dreaming about ? Why , I ’ m the owner of all these things , [*] bought with my own savings .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1188b
sections.translation: Those , you mean , which the upper part of your thighs supports [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1189_1190
sections.original: Vncti hi sunt senes , fricari sese ex antiquo volunt .
sections.translation: These old fellows have been anointed ; they want themselves rubbed down [*] , after the old-fashioned custom .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1191
sections.original: Responde , opsecro hercle , vero serio hoc quod te rogo :
sections.translation: I ’ faith , prithee , answer me this truly and seriously , which I ask of you : What are you to earn ? For how trifling a sum has Pseudolus hired you ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1193
sections.translation: Who is this Pseudolus ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1193b
sections.original: Praeceptor tuos , qui te hanc fallaciam
sections.translation: Your tutor , who has instructed you in this knavery , to take away the woman hence from me by stratagem .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1195
sections.original: Quem tu Pseudolum , quas tu mihi praedicas fallacias ?
sections.translation: What Pseudolus , and what stratagem are you talking to me about ? A person that I know of no colour , black or white .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1196b
sections.translation: Will you not be off from here ? There ’ s no profit here for swindlers to-day . Therefore you may tell Pseudolus that another person has carried off the prize-the first Harpax that came .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1199b
sections.original: Is quidem edepol Harpax ego sum .
sections.translation: On my word , I really am that Harpax .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1199c
sections.translation: Aye , on my word , you want to be .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1200
sections.translation: This is nothing more nor less than a downright impostor .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1200b
sections.translation: I have given yourself the money , and a while ago , immediately on my arrival , the token to your servant ; a letter sealed with the likeness of my master , here before the door .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1203
sections.original: Meo tu epistulam dedisti servo ? quoi servo ?
sections.translation: You gave a letter to my servant ? What servant ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1203b
sections.translation: To Syrus .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1204
sections.original: Non confidit sycophanta hic nequam est nugis : meditatus malest .
sections.translation: The wicked rogue has assurance enough [*] . He hasn ’ t contrived his knavish scheme amiss .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1205
sections.original: edepol hominem verberonem Pseudolum , ut docte dolum
sections.translation: By my faith , that whipping-post of a fellow , Pseudolus , how cleverly he has managed his plans ; just as much money as the Captain owed , he has given this man , and has dressed out the fellow that he might take away the woman ⋯ ( To HARPAX .) But the real Harpax himself brought that letter hither to me .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1209_1210
sections.original: Harpax ego vocor , ego servos sum Macedonis militis ;
sections.translation: My name is Harpax ; I am the servant of the Macedonian Captain . I ’ m doing nothing roguishly or cheatingly , nor do I know or understand this Pseudolus , what mortal being he is .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1213
sections.original: Tu , nisi mirumst , leno , plane perdidisti mulierem .
sections.translation: Unless it ’ s something wonderful , procurer , you ’ ve clearly lost the woman .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1214
sections.original: Edepol ne istuc magis magisque metuo , quom verba audio .
sections.translation: Assuredly , by my troth , I ’ m in dread of that more and more , when I come to hear his words .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1215
sections.original: mihi quoque edepol iamdudum ille Surus cor perfrigefacit ,
sections.translation: I ’ faith , that Syrus , too , has already set my heart a-freezing , that received the token from him . [*]

sections.label: Section 4.7.1217
sections.original: eho tu , qua facie fuit , dúdum quoi dedisti sumbolum ?
sections.translation: ( To HARPAX .) How now , you ! of what appearance was the person to whom you delivered the token ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1218
sections.original: Rufus quidam , ventriosus , crassis suris , subniger ,
sections.translation: A certain red-haired fellow [*] , pot-bellied , with thick calves , swarthy , with a big head , sharp eyes , red face ,

sections.label: Section 4.7.1220
sections.translation: and very large feet .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1220b
sections.translation: You prove our undoing , when you mention the feet . It was Pseudolus himself . It ’ s all up with me . I ’ m dying now , Simo .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1222
sections.original: Hercle te hau sinam emoriri , nisi mi argentum redditur ,
sections.translation: By my troth , I shan ’ t let you die , unless the money ’ s returned me-twenty minae .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1223b
sections.original: Atque etiam mihi aliae viginti minae .
sections.translation: And another twenty minae to me as well .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1224
sections.original: Auferen tu id praemium a me , quod promisi per iocum ?
sections.translation: And is the sum to be taken of me that I promised by way of a joke ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1225
sections.original: De improbis viris auferri praemium et praedam decet .
sections.translation: From unprincipled men it ’ s proper for both their money and their plunder to be taken .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1226
sections.translation: At least you might give up Pseudolus to me .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1226b
sections.translation: I , give up Pseudolus to you ? What has he done amiss ? Did I not tell you a hundred times that you were to beware of him ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1228
sections.translation: He has ruined me .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1228b
sections.original: At me viginti modicis multavit minis .
sections.translation: And on me he has laid a fine of twenty fair minae .
